topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly above the keyboard. The headline read exactly as I had written it, the nuanced argument I’d poured weeks into—but the byline was wrong. It belonged to a stranger. A complete stranger who apparently possessed my words. I reread the text, the familiar phrasing now feeling alien, tainted. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the wooden floor. Who was this person? How could they have taken my voice and draped it over their own name? I stared at the screen, the blue light reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes, a profound sense of disorientation settling deep within my chest.

[story 2]

It was the sheer audacity of it that made my jaw lock. I had spent so much time crafting that piece, pouring my anxieties and insights onto the page, only to see it floating under someone else’s signature. A slow, burning heat began to rise in my cheeks, making me want to physically shrink away from the monitor. I started pacing the small apartment, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming of my heart. Every time I touched my phone, I felt a prickle of dread, as if I were waiting for the inevitable confirmation of this deception. "No," I whispered into the empty room, the sound thin and brittle. My hands began to shake uncontrollably as I tried to reconcile the reality of the published article with the reality of my own effort. It felt like a fundamental piece of myself had been misplaced, stolen.

[story 3]

I reread the publication confirmation twice, my breath catching in shallow gasps. The realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, creeping paralysis. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the mismatched names on the page. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to look out at the indifferent city street below. The noise of traffic seemed too loud, too aggressive. I felt a desperate need to scream, but no sound emerged. My shoulders slumped, a heavy defeat settling over me. This wasn't just a mistake; it felt like a deliberate erasure, a violation of the trust I placed in the process. I slowly turned back to the computer, my movements heavy, each step costing an immense effort.
